title: Deep Learning for Optical Flow (FlowNet)
concept: FlowNet uses convolutional encoder-decoder neural networks to predict dense optical flow directly from adjacent video frames.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main difference between sparse and dense optical flow?

options.text: Sparse tracks select points; dense tracks every pixel
Sparse only works on color video; dense is for grayscale
Dense is faster to compute than sparse

exercises.explain: Yes! Sparse flow focuses on keypoints (saving time), while dense flow maps the entire image.

cards.title: Letting the network guess
cards.body: For decades, computer scientists hand-crafted complex mathematical equations to calculate optical flow, hoping the brightness wouldn't change. But what if we just let a neural network look at two frames and learn to predict the motion vectors itself?

cards.title: FlowNet
cards.body: FlowNet was the first end-to-end convolutional network designed to predict optical flow. It takes two stacked frames as input, passes them through a CNN encoder to compress features, and uses a decoder to output a dense flow vector field.

cards.title: A spot-the-difference expert
cards.body: Imagine hiring an artist who is an expert at spot-the-difference puzzles. You show them two photos. Instead of doing math, they look at the photos and instantly sketch out arrows showing where everything slid. FlowNet is that trained artist!

cards.title: FlowNet architectures
cards.body:
FlowNet introduced two structures:
1. FlowNetS (Simple): Stacks the two frames together as a single input.
2. FlowNetC (Corr): Processes both frames separately and uses a **Correlation Layer** to compare their feature maps.

cards.title: No physical rules needed
cards.body: Unlike classical algorithms, FlowNet doesn't assume brightness constancy! It learns from training data, allowing it to successfully predict motion even under shadow shifts, glare, and changing lights.

cards.title: Classical vs. Deep Optical Flow
cards.body: Comparing classical methods and deep learning approaches:

left.text:
Uses hand-crafted calculus
Sensitive to lighting changes
No training data required

right.text:
Uses a trained neural network
Robust to lighting and shadows
Requires massive training data

exercises.prompt: What is FlowNet designed to predict?

options.text: Dense optical flow from two adjacent frames
The resolution of the camera sensor
The frames per second of a video file
The class label of the entire video

exercises.explain: Correct! FlowNet takes two consecutive frames and outputs a dense vector map of optical flow.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Unlike classical optical flow methods, FlowNet does not rely on the brightness constancy assumption.
exercises.explain: True. FlowNet learns feature matching from data, which allows it to handle changes in lighting.

exercises.sentence: FlowNetC uses a ___ layer to explicitly compute matching similarities between the feature maps of two frames.
exercises.explain: The correlation layer compares spatial locations in two feature maps to detect matching parts.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a variant of the original FlowNet architecture?
exercises.explain: FlowNetS is the simple version that stacks input frames directly together.

exercises.prompt: Why does FlowNetC perform better than FlowNetS on videos with large displacement movements?

options.text: Because its correlation layer explicitly calculates spatial similarities across search windows.
Because it automatically runs at a higher FPS rate.
Because FlowNetS ignores the spatial dimensions entirely.
Because it runs only on grayscale videos.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The correlation layer in FlowNetC mathematically matches features across a search window, allowing it to track objects that jumped a long distance between frames. Next: how do we classify complete video categories? Next up: Video Classification Architectures.
